content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

August 24, 1976

Mr. Peter C. Carton
Deputy County Counsel
Office of the County Counsel
1415 Truxtun Avenue
Bakersfield, California 93301

Dear Mr. Carton:

Thank you for your letter of July 15, 1976, asking whether
a Health Council established under Public Law 93-641 falls with-
in the definition of a "local government agency" under Government Code §82041, and your subsequent letter of July 30, 1976,
with attached U.S. Code materials and the amended by-laws of
the Central California Health Systems Agency.

Public Law 93-641 provides for the creation of a health
systems agency, which has as its primary responsibility the
provision of effective health planning for its health service
area and the promotion and development within the area of health
services, manpower, and facilities which meet identified needs,
reduce documented inefficiencies, and implement the health plans
of the agency.

The provisions of Public Law 93-641 control the functions,
mode of operation, legal structure, staff, composition and res-
ponsibility of governing boards, and the funding of a health
systems agency. Though the provisions of the statute permit
a health systems agency to take the legal form of a private,
non-profit corporation, established pursuant to the laws of
the respective state, the essential provisions governing the
existence and operation of a health systems agency are dictated
by federal, not state and local law.

Public Law 93-641 further provides that a health systems
agency may establish sub-area advisory councils, representing
parts of the agencies' health service area, to advise the governing board of the agency on the performance of its functions.
Although these local health councils possess some characteristics
of a local government agency -- e.g., creation and appointment
by the county board of supervisors -- their authority, functions,

Mr. Peter C. Carton
August 24, 1976
Page Two

mode of operation, and the composition of their membership,
are all dictated by federal statute. The provisions of the
federal statute are duplicated and cited in by-laws of
the Central California Health Systems Agency, which govern
the actions of the health council in your county. Further,
the primary function of the health council is to advise its
parent body, a health systems agency that itself (as pointed
out above) is a creature of federal law, though clothed in
the form of a private, non-profit corporation. Accordingly,
it is our opinion that a health council, established pursuant
to the provisions of Public Law 93-641, is not a local government agency within the meaning of Government Code §82041.

With regard to the applicability of conflict of interest
provisions, I am informed by staff in the Health Planning
Branch, Region IX, HEW, in San Francisco (415/556-3550), that
health systems agency board members and health council members alike are subject to federal conflict of interest provisions that substantially mirror the provisions of California's
conflict of interest statute. You may wish to explore in more
detail the precise nature of these federal conflict of interest
provisions, and the federal disclaimer statements that are
required to be executed.

If I can be of further assistance to you, please let me
know.

Sincerely,

Edgar A. Kerry
Staff Attorney
Conflicts of Interest
Division

EAK:gb

OFFICE OF THE

COUNTY COUNSEL

COUNTY OF KERN
1415 Trustun Avenue
Bakersfield, California
93301

Room 702
Administrative Court Building

Telephone (805) 861-2326

RALPH B. JORDAN
County Counsel

July 15, 1976

In reply please refer
to Our File Number:

Del Spurlock, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street Bldg.
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Application of Proposition 9 to Health
Councils established under PL 96-341

Dear Mr. Spurlock:

A question has been raised as to whether a Health Council
established under PL 96-341 (as amplified by Title 42, Chapter 1,
Part 122 et seq. in Federal Register, Vol. 41, No. 60) falls
within the definition of a local public agency under Government
Code §82041.

May we ask if the Commission has rendered an opinion on
this point? If not, may we ask that you consider the matter
and let us know when you have reached a decision?

Thank you.

Very truly yours,

RALPH B. JORDAN, County Counsel

By: [signature]
Peter C. Carton
Deputy County Counsel

July 15, 1976

In reply please refer
to Our File Number:


Del Spurlock, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street Bldg.
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Application of Proposition 9 to Health
Councils established under PL 96-341

Dear Mr. Spurlock:

A question has been raised as to whether a Health Council
established under PL 96-341 (as amplified by Title 42, Chapter 1,
Part 122 et seq. in Federal Register, Vol. 41, No. 60) falls
within the definition of a local public agency under Government
Code §82041.

May we ask if the Commission has rendered an opinion on
this point? If not, may we ask that you consider the matter
and let us know when you have reached a decision?

Thank you.

Very truly yours,

RALPH B. JORDAN, County Counsel

By [signature]
Peter C. Carton
Deputy County Counsel

sections.question: Does a Health Council established under Public Law 93-641 fall within the definition of a 'local government agency' under Government Code §82041?
sections.conclusion: It is our opinion that a health council, established pursuant to the provisions of Public Law 93-641, is not a local government agency within the meaning of Government Code §82041.
sections.question_synthetic: The County Counsel of Kern County asks whether Health Councils established under Public Law 93-641 (the National Health Planning and Resources Development Act) constitute 'local government agencies' under California Government Code §82041 for purposes of Proposition 9 compliance.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that Health Councils established under Public Law 93-641 are not local government agencies under Government Code §82041 because their authority, functions, mode of operation, and membership composition are dictated by federal statute rather than state or local law, even though they may possess some characteristics of local government agencies.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter (July 15, 1976) from County Counsel and the FPPC response letter (August 24, 1976) from Edgar A. Kerry. The FPPC response clearly identifies the question and provides a definitive conclusion. Minor discrepancy in cited statute: incoming letter references PL 96-341, but FPPC response and document ID reference PL 93-641 (correct citation).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does a Health Council established under Public Law 93-641 fall within the definition of a 'local government agency' under Government Code §82041?

CONCLUSION: It is our opinion that a health council, established pursuant to the provisions of Public Law 93-641, is not a local government agency within the meaning of Government Code §82041.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that Health Councils established under Public Law 93-641 are not 'local government agencies' under Government Code §82041 because they are creatures of federal law. The FPPC notes that Health Council members are subject to federal conflict of interest provisions that substantially mirror California's conflict of interest statute.
